name: Spinach (Palak)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Steam lightly or sauté with a pinch of cumin and ghee for 3–5 minutes. Avoid overcooking.

prakriti.vata: aggravating
prakriti.kapha: pacifying

name: Meetha Neem (Moringa Leaves / Sahjan Ke Patte)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 4–6 minutes until wilted and fragrant. Can be added to dal, khichdi, or made into a light soup. Avoid overcooking to preserve nutrients.

prakriti.vata: aggravating if eaten dry or raw; pacifying when cooked with ghee and spices
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (bitter + heating); use in moderation during high pitta
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for clearing mucus, boosting metabolism, and reducing fat)

name: Chana Chana (Whole Chickpea Curry)
ingredients: 1 cup dried whole chickpeas (chana), soaked overnight (approx. 200g)
instructions: Soak whole chickpeas overnight and drain. In a pressure cooker or pot, add soaked chickpeas and enough water to cover; cook until tender (about 3–4 whistles or 45–60 minutes on stove). In a separate pan, heat ghee. Add cumin seeds and let them sizzle. Add hing, ginger, green chili, and sauté for 30 seconds. Add onions and cook until golden. Stir in tomatoes, turmeric, coriander, and cumin powders; cook until oil separates. Add cooked chickpeas with some of their cooking water. Simmer for 10–15 minutes to blend flavors. Garnish with fresh coriander if desired. Serve warm with rice or flatbread.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (especially if undercooked or eaten in excess; better when well-cooked with digestive spices)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (due to heaviness and bulk)

name: Masoor Dal (Red Lentil Curry)
ingredients: 1 cup whole or split red masoor dal (approx. 200g)
instructions: Rinse masoor dal thoroughly until water runs clear. In a pot, combine dal and water; bring to a boil, then simmer for 15–20 minutes (or 3–4 whistles in a pressure cooker) until soft and mushy. In a separate pan, heat ghee. Add cumin seeds and let them sizzle. Add hing, ginger, and green chili; sauté 30 seconds. Add onions and cook until translucent. Stir in tomatoes, turmeric, and coriander powder; cook until oil begins to separate. Add the cooked dal and mix well. Simmer for 5–10 minutes to blend flavors. Adjust consistency with warm water if needed. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve warm with rice or roti.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (especially when cooked well with digestive spices)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (due to mild heaviness)

name: Idli (Fermented Rice & Urad Dal Steamed Cakes)
ingredients: 1.5 cups idli rice or parboiled rice
instructions: Soak urad dal and rice separately for 4–6 hours. Grind urad dal with fenugreek to a light, fluffy batter. Grind rice to a slightly coarse texture. Mix both batters with salt, cover, and ferment in a warm place for 8–12 hours until doubled in volume. Stir gently, pour into greased idli molds, and steam for 10–12 minutes. Cool slightly before unmolding. Serve with coconut chutney or sambar.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (when well-fermented and eaten warm with ghee or digestive accompaniments)
prakriti.kapha: slightly aggravating in excess (due to heaviness from fermentation and rice); best eaten in moderation with ginger or black pepper

name: Dhokla (Steamed Fermented Chickpea Flour Snack)
ingredients: 1/2 tsp baking soda or Eno fruit salt (for instant version)
Lemon juice or fresh coriander for garnish (optional)
instructions: Mix besan, salt, turmeric, ginger-chili paste, and enough water to make a smooth, thick batter. For fermented version, let sit 4–6 hours in warm place; for instant, skip fermentation and use Eno/baking soda. Just before steaming, add baking soda or Eno and mix quickly. Pour into greased steamer tray and steam for 15–20 minutes. Let cool slightly, then cut into diamonds. Heat oil, temper with mustard seeds, sesame seeds, and curry leaves. Pour over dhokla. Garnish with coriander or lemon juice. Serve warm or at room temperature.

prakriti.vata: neutral to slightly aggravating (due to dryness of besan); balance with ghee or coconut chutney
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, dry, and steamed—ideal in moderation)

name: Poha (Flattened Rice Stir-Fry)
ingredients: Fresh coriander and lemon juice for garnish
Water or warm milk for rinsing poha (minimal)
instructions: Rinse poha quickly under water and drain well—do not soak. Heat ghee in a pan. Add mustard and cumin seeds; let them pop. Add curry leaves, ginger, green chili, and sauté 30 seconds. Add onion and potato (if using); cook until soft. Stir in turmeric and salt. Add drained poha, mix gently, and cook 2–3 minutes on low heat. Garnish with coriander and lemon juice. Serve warm.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (due to dryness); balance with ghee and warm spices like cumin
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and dry—ideal in moderation)

name: Rajma (Kidney Bean Curry)
ingredients: 1 cup dried kidney beans (rajma), soaked overnight
instructions: Soak rajma overnight (8–12 hours). Drain and pressure cook with fresh water until very soft (4–5 whistles or 45–60 mins). In a pan, heat ghee. Add cumin seeds and hing; sauté 30 seconds. Add onions, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste; cook 2 mins. Stir in tomato puree, turmeric, coriander, and cumin powders; cook until oil separates. Add cooked rajma with some broth. Simmer 10–15 mins. Finish with garam masala and coriander. Serve hot with rice.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (heavy and hard to digest; best with digestive spices like ginger, hing, and cumin)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (due to heaviness and bulk); consume in small portions

name: Arhar Dal (Split Pigeon Pea Curry)
instructions: Wash toor dal thoroughly. Cook with water and turmeric in a pressure cooker for 3–4 whistles or until soft and mushy. In a separate pan, heat ghee. Add mustard and cumin seeds; let them splutter. Add hing, then onions (if using), and sauté until golden. Add tomatoes and spices; cook until oil separates. Pour in cooked dal, mix well, and simmer 5–7 minutes. Garnish and serve warm with rice or roti.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (one of the most vata-friendly dals when well-cooked with ghee and hing)
prakriti.kapha: neutral (lighter than other dals; fine in moderation)

name: Groundnut (Roasted Peanuts)
ingredients: 1 cup raw peanuts (with or without skin)
Pinch of rock salt (sendha namak) or sea salt (optional)
instructions: Dry roast raw peanuts in a heavy pan on low-medium heat, stirring constantly, until golden and fragrant (8–10 mins). Alternatively, roast in oven at 180°C (350°F) for 12–15 mins. Cool, then rub off skins if desired. Lightly sprinkle with salt and turmeric. Store in an airtight container. Consume in moderation (1–2 tbsp per serving).

prakriti.vata: pacifying (oily and nourishing—excellent in small amounts)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (due to heating potency and oiliness); avoid in excess or during summer
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, oily, and dense); consume sparingly or avoid

name: Carrot (Gajar)
ingredients: 1 medium carrot, peeled and sliced or grated (approx. 70g)
instructions: Wash thoroughly and peel if not organic. Eat raw for crispness and maximum nutrients, or steam/sauté lightly in ghee with a pinch of cumin for 5–7 minutes until tender-crisp. Avoid prolonged cooking to preserve beta-carotene and vitamin C.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (sweet and grounding; best cooked with ghee)
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (due to heating virya); balance with cooling herbs like coriander or eat raw in moderation
prakriti.kapha: slightly aggravating in excess (sweet taste increases kapha); best consumed cooked and spiced with ginger or black pepper

name: Onion (Pyaaz)
instructions: Peel and slice, chop, or grate as needed. For raw use (e.g., in salads), soak briefly in water or lemon juice to reduce sharpness. For cooking, sauté in ghee or oil with cumin or mustard seeds until golden—5–8 minutes. Can be used as a base for dals, curries, or stir-fries. Avoid excessive frying to prevent pitta aggravation.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (unctuous and grounding; especially when cooked)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (due to pungent taste and heating potency); best used in small amounts or well-cooked with cooling spices like coriander
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, sweet, and slimy qualities increase kapha); use sparingly and balance with ginger, black pepper, or turmeric

name: Lahsun (Garlic)
instructions: Peel garlic cloves. Use raw for maximum medicinal potency (e.g., in chutneys or infused honey), or sauté gently in ghee or oil for 1–2 minutes to mellow its sharpness. Avoid overcooking or high-heat frying, which can create irritants. Often used as a flavor base in dals, soups, and kitchari.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (warm, oily, and grounding; excellent for circulation and digestion)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (strong heating and sharp qualities); use in very small amounts or avoid in excess, especially during summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (stimulates digestion, clears congestion, and reduces stagnation)

name: Beans (Green Beans / French Beans)
ingredients: 1 cup fresh green beans, trimmed and chopped (approx. 100g)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Steam or sauté in ghee with a pinch of cumin and turmeric for 5–7 minutes until tender-crisp. Avoid overcooking to retain nutrients and texture.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (due to dryness); balance with ghee and spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and diuretic)

name: Sesame Seeds (Til)
ingredients: 1 tbsp raw or lightly roasted white sesame seeds (approx. 10g)
instructions: Dry roast on low heat for 2–3 minutes until golden and fragrant. Cool and store in an airtight container. Use in chutneys, laddoos, or sprinkle over vegetables. Best consumed with jaggery or dates for balance.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (warm, oily, nourishing—ideal in winter)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (heating); use sparingly in summer
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and oily); consume in very small amounts

name: Lal Bhaji (Red Amaranth Greens)
ingredients: 1 cup fresh red amaranth leaves, chopped (approx. 40g)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, a pinch of asafoetida, and green chili for 4–6 minutes until wilted but still vibrant. Add a splash of water if needed. Avoid overcooking.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and rough qualities); always cook with ghee and digestive spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and detoxifying)

name: Apple (Seb)
instructions: Eat raw with skin (if organic) for fiber. For vata balance, stew with cinnamon and a drop of ghee. Best consumed between meals—not with dairy or heavy meals.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating raw (dry quality); pacifying when cooked with warming spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (astringent quality helps reduce kapha)

name: Orange (Santra)
instructions: Eat fresh segments between meals. Avoid combining with dairy or starches. Best consumed alone for optimal digestion.

prakriti.vata: neutral to slightly aggravating (sour can stimulate vata if excessive); consume in moderation
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (due to sour taste and citric acid); limit during pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and detoxifying; excellent in morning)

name: Wheat Flour (Whole Wheat Atta)
instructions: Use to make roti, paratha, or porridge. Always combine with digestive spices (ajwain, cumin) or ghee for easier digestion. Avoid if gluten-sensitive.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (grounding and nourishing)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and dense); best avoided or used minimally in kapha imbalance

name: Rice Flour (Chawal ka Atta)
instructions: Use for gluten-free dosa, pancakes, or thickening soups. Lighter than wheat but still starchy—best combined with lentils or vegetables for balance.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (dry); balance with ghee or coconut milk
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (starchy); use in moderation

name: Corn Flour (Makai ka Atta)
instructions: Use to make roti, porridge, or as a thickener. Best consumed warm with ghee and digestive spices like black pepper or ginger to aid digestion.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (rough and drying); use only with ample ghee and warm liquids
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating potency); avoid in summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating (heavy); consume in small amounts

name: Pea (Matar)
ingredients: 1/2 cup fresh or frozen green peas (approx. 75g)
instructions: Steam or sauté in ghee with cumin, ginger, and a pinch of turmeric for 5–7 minutes. Avoid overcooking. Can be added to rice, dals, or stir-fries.

prakriti.vata: neutral to slightly aggravating (heavy); balance with digestive spices
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (sweet and heavy); use sparingly

name: Corn (Makai / Sweet Corn)
ingredients: 1 cob fresh corn or 1/2 cup corn kernels (approx. 85g)
instructions: Boil or steam kernels for 5–7 minutes. Alternatively, sauté in ghee with black pepper and salt. Best eaten warm and not combined with dairy.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (rough and hard to digest); consume only with ghee and digestive spices
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating); avoid in excess during summer
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (sweet and starchy); limit intake

name: Cabbage (Patta Gobi)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Lightly sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 5–7 minutes until tender-crisp. Avoid raw consumption if vata is imbalanced.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and gassy); always cook with ghee and digestive spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and detoxifying)

name: Radish (Mooli)
instructions: Peel if skin is tough. Eat raw in small amounts in salads, or better yet, sauté or steam with cumin, black pepper, and a touch of ghee for 6–8 minutes. Avoid excess raw consumption.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (drying and stimulating); use only cooked with oil/spices
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating and pungent); avoid in excess or during pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (cleansing, decongestant, and metabolic stimulant)

name: Lemon (Nimbu)
instructions: Use fresh juice in warm water (not hot) for digestion, or add a few drops to cooked dishes at the end. Avoid combining with milk or yogurt.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in small amounts (sour taste grounds vata)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (sour increases acidity); use very moderately
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (stimulates digestion and clears congestion)

name: Beetroot (Chukandar)
ingredients: 1 medium beetroot, peeled and grated or cubed (approx. 100g)
instructions: Steam or roast until tender (20–25 mins). Can be eaten warm with cumin and ghee. Raw beet juice is potent—use sparingly and only in pitta-balanced individuals.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moist, grounding)
prakriti.kapha: slightly aggravating (heavy and sweet); consume in moderation

name: Cauliflower (Gobi)
instructions: Wash and steam or sauté in ghee with turmeric, ginger, and cumin for 6–8 minutes. Avoid overcooking. Best served warm with digestive spices.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and gassy); always cook well with oil and spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and detoxifying)

name: Potato (Aloo)
instructions: Boil, steam, or sauté in ghee with cumin, turmeric, and a pinch of hing for 10–12 minutes. Avoid frying. Eat warm and in moderation.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (grounding and moist)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, sweet, and starchy); limit intake

name: Sweet Potato (Shakarkandi)
ingredients: 1 medium sweet potato, peeled and cubed (approx. 130g)
instructions: Steam or bake until tender (20–25 mins). For balance, add a pinch of cinnamon or black pepper. Avoid excessive oil or sugar.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (nourishing and grounding)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (sweet + heating); consume moderately
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (sweet and heavy); best avoided during kapha imbalance

name: Tomato (Tamatar)
instructions: Best used cooked—sauté in ghee with spices until oil separates. Raw use may aggravate pitta; avoid combining with dairy or cucumber.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in cooked form (moist and sour)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (sour + heating; increases acidity); minimize or avoid
prakriti.kapha: slightly aggravating (slimy and heavy); use in small cooked amounts

name: Green Methi (Fresh Fenugreek Leaves)
instructions: Wash well. Sauté in ghee with cumin, turmeric, and a pinch of hing for 4–6 minutes until reduced and aromatic. Avoid raw use due to bitterness.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and bitter); always cook with ghee and spices
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (reduces fat, clears mucus, stimulates metabolism)

name: Methi Seeds (Fenugreek Seeds)
instructions: Dry roast lightly to reduce bitterness, or soak overnight and consume on empty stomach for blood sugar support. More commonly used as a spice—temper 1/4–1/2 tsp in hot oil before adding other ingredients.

prakriti.vata: aggravating in excess (drying); balance with ghee or milk if used medicinally
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (bitter pacifies, but heating may irritate); use moderately
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for metabolism, digestion, and clearing ama)

name: Kaju (Cashew Nuts)
instructions: Soak in water for 2–4 hours to improve digestibility. Consume raw, blend into milk, or use in savory sauces. Avoid frying or excessive roasting.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (nourishing and grounding)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (heating and oily); limit intake in summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and sweet); consume in very small amounts

name: Kishmish (Golden Raisins)
instructions: Soak in warm water for 10–15 minutes to plump. Eat alone, add to milk, or use in rice dishes. Best consumed mid-morning for energy.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (lubricating and nutritive)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (sweet and heavy); use sparingly or with ginger/saunf

name: Anjeer (Dried Fig)
instructions: Soak overnight in water. Consume in the morning on empty stomach or blend into warm milk. Excellent for digestion and elimination.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (moist, grounding, and laxative in nature)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and sweet); consume only occasionally and with digestive spices

name: Munakka (Black Raisins)
instructions: Soak in warm water for 15 minutes. Eat directly or add to desserts. Often used in Ayurvedic remedies for anemia and weakness.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (nourishing and blood-building)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (sweet and dense); best taken with black pepper or saunf

name: Badam (Almond)
instructions: Soak overnight, peel skin in the morning, and eat on empty stomach. Can be ground into paste for milk or desserts. Avoid dry/raw consumption.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (nourishes nerves and tissues)
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (heating); peel skin to reduce pitta effect
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and oily); consume in strict moderation

name: Akhrot (Walnut)
instructions: Soak for 4–6 hours to reduce astringency. Eat in the morning or add to warm milk. Best consumed during colder months.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (excellent for brain, nerves, and dryness)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating and bitter); avoid in excess or during summer
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy and oily); use only in small doses

name: Saunf (Fennel Seeds)
instructions: Chew raw after meals to aid digestion and freshen breath. Or steep in hot water for 5–10 minutes to make a soothing tea. Can be dry roasted for enhanced flavor.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (carminative and calming)
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light and clears mucus)

name: Soonth (Dry Ginger Powder)
instructions: Mix with honey, warm water, or milk for digestion. Add to teas, dals, or kitchari. Avoid during high pitta or inflammation.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (warms digestion and circulation)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (strong heating effect); avoid in excess or during summer
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for clearing congestion and stimulating agni)

name: Besan Cheela
instructions: Mix besan, salt, turmeric, hing, and cumin in a bowl. Add chopped veggies and ginger-chili paste. Gradually add water to make a flowing (not watery) batter. Rest 10 minutes. Heat pan, pour ladle of batter, spread thin. Drizzle ghee, cook 2–3 mins per side until golden. Serve with green chutney.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (dry); must use ghee and avoid excess raw onion
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (light, dry, warm when cooked)

name: Moong Dal Cheela
ingredients: 1 cup split yellow moong dal, soaked 3–4 hours
instructions: Drain soaked moong dal. Blend with minimal water to a smooth, thick batter. Mix in spices, veggies, and salt. Rest 10 mins. Heat pan, spread thin layer, drizzle ghee, cook 2–3 mins each side. Soft, digestible, and nourishing.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (easiest to digest dal, grounding)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess; add black pepper or ginger to balance

name: Oats Cheela
ingredients: 1 tsp ghee or coconut oil per cheela
instructions: Blend oats with water to a smooth paste. Add veggies, spices, and salt. Rest 5–10 mins. Heat pan, spread batter thin, cook 2–3 mins per side with ghee. Crispy edges, soft center.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (dry/rough); always pair with ghee or chutney
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, warming when spiced)

name: Vegetable Cheela
ingredients: 3/4 cup besan or moong dal batter (prepared as above)
1/2 cup mixed finely chopped veggies (carrot, bell pepper, spinach, onion, cabbage)
instructions: Prepare base batter (besan or moong). Mix in all veggies and spices. Adjust consistency. Cook on medium heat with ghee until crisp and cooked through. Best served fresh.

prakriti.vata: neutral if moong-based + ghee; aggravating if besan + raw cabbage/onion
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, spiced, warm)

name: Whole Moong Cheela (Hari Moong Cheela)
ingredients: 1 cup whole green moong (with skin), soaked 4–6 hours
2 tbsp chopped fenugreek leaves (methi) or spinach
instructions: Drain soaked whole moong. Blend with minimal water to a coarse, textured batter (do not over-blend). Mix in spices and veggies. Rest 10 mins. Cook on medium heat with ghee until both sides golden. Hearty, fiber-rich, and deeply nourishing.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (rough, hard to digest); use only with ample ghee, cumin, and hing
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (but limit if digestion is weak)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating (heavy); best with extra ginger and black pepper

name: Multigrain Roti
ingredients: 1 tbsp ground flaxseed or roasted chia
instructions: Mix all flours, ajwain, and salt. Gradually add warm water to form a soft, pliable dough. Rest 15–20 minutes. Divide into balls, roll into thin rotis. Cook on a hot tawa until golden spots appear on both sides. Apply ghee if desired. Serve warm with dal or sabzi.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (nourishing, warm when fresh)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy); best eaten in small portions or without wheat

name: Millet Roti (Jowar or Bajra Bhakri)
ingredients: 1 cup jowar (sorghum) or bajra (pearl millet) flour
instructions: In a bowl, mix millet flour and salt. Add ghee. Gradually pour hot water while stirring until dough forms (should be soft but firm). Knead well. Divide into balls, flatten by hand (not rolling pin). Cook on tawa, pressing gently with a cloth, until charred spots appear. Best served hot with ghee and onion.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry/rough); must eat hot with ghee or sesame oil
prakriti.pitta: jowar = pacifying; bajra = aggravating in summer
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (especially jowar—light, warming, metabolic)

name: Oats & Moong Dal Bread(Roti)
ingredients: 1/2 cup yellow moong dal flour (or soaked & ground moong)
1 tbsp flaxseed gel (1 tsp flax + 3 tbsp water, soaked 10 mins)
instructions: Mix dry ingredients. Add flax gel, grated veg, and enough water to form thick batter. Add baking soda last. Pour into greased loaf pan. Steam for 25–30 mins or bake at 180°C (350°F) for 30–35 mins. Cool before slicing. Eat warm with chutney.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moong + oats = grounding)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating; add ginger/black pepper to balance

name: Sprouted Grain Bread(Roti)
ingredients: 1 cup mixed sprouted grains (wheat, moong, moth beans), drained
2 tbsp ground sunflower or pumpkin seeds
instructions: Grind sprouted grains to a coarse paste. Add psyllium husk, seeds, spices, and salt. Mix well—dough should be thick and sticky. Rest 10 mins (psyllium will absorb moisture). Transfer to greased loaf pan. Bake at 175°C (350°F) for 40–45 mins. Cool completely before slicing. Store refrigerated.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (sprouting reduces gas, adds enzymes)
prakriti.kapha: neutral; best in moderation due to density

name: Coconut & Rice Flour Flatbread(Roti)
ingredients: 1/4 cup tapioca starch or arrowroot (optional, for pliability)
instructions: Mix rice flour, coconut, tapioca, salt, and cumin. Add hot water and oil; stir into soft dough. Rest 10 mins. Divide into balls, roll between parchment paper. Cook on medium-hot tawa 2–3 mins per side until firm and slightly crisp. Best served fresh with coconut chutney.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (coconut oil adds moisture); avoid if too dry
prakriti.kapha: aggravating in excess (coconut is heavy); use sparingly

name: Egg (Anda Bhurji)
instructions: Beat eggs with salt and turmeric. Heat ghee in a pan. Add cumin and hing; sizzle 10 seconds. Add onions, sauté until soft. Add tomatoes (if using), cook 1 minute. Pour in egg mixture. Stir gently until softly set (2–3 minutes). Do not overcook. Garnish with coriander. Serve warm with roti or alone.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (nourishing, grounding)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (heating and dense); eat occasionally, avoid fried or spicy versions
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, increases mucus); consume only in small amounts, well-spiced with ginger/black pepper

name: Chicken (Ayurvedic Chicken Curry)
instructions: Heat ghee. Add cumin, coriander seeds, cardamom, and hing; sizzle 20 seconds. Add onions, cook until golden. Add ginger-garlic paste; sauté 1 minute. Add tomatoes and spices; cook until oil separates. Add chicken, mix well. Cover and cook on low heat 12–15 minutes, stirring occasionally. Add minimal water if needed. Finish with fennel powder and coriander. Serve with rice or millet roti.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (especially dark meat with ghee and warming spices)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating and dense); consume rarely, with cooling sides like cucumber raita
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, builds tissue/mucus); eat only occasionally, well-spiced with black pepper, ginger, and minimal oil

name: Meat (Goat/Lamb Curry)
ingredients: 300g goat or lamb meat (bone-in preferred for flavor)
instructions: Wash and pat-dry meat. In a heavy pot, heat ghee. Add whole spices (cumin, fenugreek, cloves, cardamom) and hing; sizzle 30 seconds. Add onions; cook until deep golden. Add ginger-garlic paste; sauté 2 minutes. Stir in tomato puree and dry spices; cook until oil surfaces. Add meat, coat well. Add water/broth, cover, and simmer gently for 45–60 minutes (or pressure cook 4–5 whistles) until tender. Skim excess fat if needed. Garnish and serve warm with roti or rice.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (deeply nourishing, builds strength—ideal in winter)
prakriti.pitta: strongly aggravating; avoid during inflammation, summer, or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (increases bulk and lethargy); consume only during extreme depletion, well-spiced, and in small portions

name: Alsi (Flaxseeds)
instructions: Grind fresh just before use (whole seeds pass undigested). Mix with warm water, honey, or warm milk. Can be added to doughs or smoothies. Best taken in morning on empty stomach for fiber and omega-3s.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (lubricating, nourishing, relieves dryness)
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (heating); use in moderation
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, slimy, increases mucus); avoid in excess

name: Arbi Leaves (Colocasia / Taro Leaves)
ingredients: 10–12 young arbi leaves, washed and chopped (approx. 50g)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Finely chop and sauté in ghee with cumin, turmeric, and a pinch of hing for 6–8 minutes until soft. Can be rolled into patra or added to dal. **Never eat raw**—contains calcium oxalate crystals.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry/rough); always cook with ghee and spices
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (detoxifying, clears congestion)

name: Bottle Gourd (Lauki)
instructions: Peel if skin is tough. Chop and cook with cumin, a pinch of hing, and minimal water for 8–10 minutes until soft. Can be made into a mild dal or juice (strained, with a drop of lemon). Best eaten warm.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moist, calming)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (sweet); best with black pepper or ginger

name: Mustard (Mustard Seeds)
ingredients: 1/2 tsp black or yellow mustard seeds (approx. 2g)
instructions: Use whole for tempering (tadka)—heat in ghee/oil until they pop. Adds pungency to dals and vegetables. Can be ground into paste (with turmeric) for topical use or cooking. Avoid raw consumption in large amounts.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (drying, sharp); use only in small amounts as spice
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (strong heating); avoid in excess or summer
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (stimulates digestion, clears mucus)

name: Soya (Soybean)
ingredients: 1/2 cup cooked soybeans or rehydrated soy chunks (approx. 70g)
instructions: Soak dried soybeans 8–12 hours, then pressure cook until soft (4–5 whistles). For chunks, soak in hot water 15 mins, squeeze out water, then cook in spiced gravy. Always cook thoroughly—never raw.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, gassy, hard to digest); use only well-cooked with hing, cumin, and ghee
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, builds bulk); consume rarely and well-spiced

name: Ridge Gourd (Turai)
instructions: Peel if mature. Chop and sauté in ghee with cumin, turmeric, and green chili for 6–8 minutes. Can be cooked with lentils or tomatoes. Avoid overcooking—retain slight firmness.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating if raw; pacifying when cooked with ghee
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, clears mucus)

name: Guava (Amrood)
ingredients: 1 medium guava, washed (with or without skin) (approx. 60g)
instructions: Eat fresh between meals. Skin is rich in fiber—consume if organic. Avoid combining with dairy or heavy meals. Best in morning for digestion.

prakriti.vata: aggravating in excess (astringent dries tissues); eat ripe, with a drop of honey if needed
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (astringent, reduces mucus, supports metabolism)

name: Drumstick (Sahjan)
instructions: Wash, chop into 2-inch pieces. Cook in sambar, dal, or light stew for 10–15 minutes until tender (skin is edible, pulp is scooped out). Do not overcook—retain nutrients.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, light); balance with ghee or coconut milk in sambar
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating (heating); use moderately
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for joints, immunity, and clearing ama)

name: Brinjal (Baingan)
instructions: Wash, cube (keep skin on). Sauté in ghee with mustard seeds, curry leaves, turmeric, and tamarind paste for 8–10 minutes until soft. Avoid frying; steaming or stewing is better for digestion.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (heavy, slimy, hard to digest); use only well-spiced with hing and cumin
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (increases mucus); consume rarely and with strong spices

name: Turnip (Shalgam)
instructions: Peel and chop. Steam or sauté in ghee with cumin, black pepper, and a pinch of hing for 8–10 minutes. Can be added to soups or kitchari. Best eaten warm.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, rough); always cook with ghee and digestive spices
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating); avoid in summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (decongestant, metabolic stimulant)

name: Capsicum (Bell Pepper)
instructions: Wash, deseed, and chop. Sauté in ghee with cumin and turmeric for 5–7 minutes until slightly soft but crisp. Avoid raw if vata is high. Never combine with milk.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, rough); cook well with oil and spices
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating); limit red/yellow varieties in summer
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, clears stagnation)

name: Papaya (Papita)
instructions: Peel, deseed, and slice ripe papaya. Eat fresh between meals—ideally in the morning on an empty stomach for optimal digestion. Avoid combining with dairy, cold drinks, or heavy meals. Unripe (green) papaya should only be used in cooked dishes like sabzi or curry.

prakriti.vata: pacifying when ripe (moist, grounding); unripe is aggravating (dry, rough)
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (cooling, supports liver); avoid excess if acidity is high
prakriti.kapha: aggravating in excess (sweet, moist); consume in small amounts, preferably with ginger or black pepper

name: Mushroom (Khumb)
instructions: Wipe clean (do not soak in water). Sauté in ghee or coconut oil with cumin seeds, grated ginger, a pinch of turmeric, and black pepper for 5–7 minutes until tender and golden. Avoid raw consumption. Best paired with light grains or vegetables—not with dairy or heavy legumes.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (heavy, difficult to digest; can increase ama); consume only occasionally, well-cooked with digestive spices
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly pacifying (cooling), but may increase toxins if digestion is weak; avoid in excess
prakriti.kapha: strongly aggravating (heavy, moist, increases mucus and stagnation); best avoided during kapha imbalance or in damp seasons

name: Broccoli (Hari Phool Gobi)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Steam for 4–5 minutes or sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida (hing) for 5–6 minutes until bright green and tender-crisp. Avoid overcooking. Best served warm, not raw—especially for vata or kapha imbalance.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, rough, and gassy); must be cooked well with ghee and digestive spices like cumin or hing
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (light, clears congestion, supports metabolism)

name: Kale (Karam Saag)
ingredients: 1 cup chopped kale leaves, stems removed (approx. 60g)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Chop finely and sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, a pinch of asafoetida (hing), and grated ginger for 5–7 minutes until wilted and tender. Adding a splash of lemon juice at the end enhances iron absorption. Avoid raw consumption, especially in vata or kapha imbalance.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and rough qualities increase vata); always cook with ghee and warming spices
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (light, clears mucus, boosts metabolism)

name: Zucchini (Jugni / Courgette)
instructions: Wash and trim ends. Leave skin on for fiber. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of hing for 5–7 minutes until tender but still firm. Can also be lightly steamed or added to kitchari. Avoid overcooking or frying.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moist and grounding, especially when cooked with ghee)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (sweet and moist); balance with ginger or black pepper

name: Yam (Jimikand / Suran)
instructions: Peel yam thoroughly (use gloves if handling raw—it can cause skin irritation). Rinse well to remove latex. Boil or steam until tender (15–20 mins), or sauté in ghee with cumin, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 10–12 minutes. Never eat raw. Best consumed warm, in moderation, and well-spiced to aid digestion.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (nourishing, warming, grounding—excellent in winter)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (heating potency); avoid during summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, sweet, increases mucus); consume only occasionally and with digestive spices like black pepper or ginger

name: Ivy Gourd / Kundru
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Slit or slice lengthwise. Sauté in ghee or oil with mustard seeds, curry leaves, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 6–8 minutes until tender. Can also be cooked with lentils or tamarind. Avoid raw consumption.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and light); always cook with ghee and digestive spices
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (cooling, supports liver and blood purification)
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for metabolism, diabetes, and clearing mucus)

name: Cluster Bean (Gawar Phali)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Trim ends and chop into 1-inch pieces. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, a pinch of asafoetida (hing), and a little grated coconut or jaggery (optional) for 7–9 minutes until tender but not mushy. Avoid overcooking to retain texture and nutrients.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry, rough, and slightly gassy); always cook with ghee and digestive spices like cumin or hing
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (light, clears mucus, beneficial for metabolism and blood sugar)

name: Parval (Pointed Gourd / Parwal)
instructions: Wash and peel if skin is tough (young parval may not need peeling). Slit lengthwise and remove seeds if mature. Chop or leave whole. Sauté in ghee with panch phoron or cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 8–10 minutes. Can be cooked with potatoes or in a light tomato-based gravy. Avoid raw consumption.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moist and grounding when cooked with ghee)
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (cooling and soothing to digestive tract)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (sweet quality); best cooked with ginger, black pepper, or pungent spices

name: Amla (Indian Gooseberry)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Remove seeds and chop or grate. Can be eaten raw with a pinch of honey and black pepper, or added to chutneys, pickles, or herbal teas. For daily use, amla powder (1/4 tsp) mixed in warm water is common. Avoid combining with milk.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in moderation (especially when combined with sesame oil or jaggery); raw amla may be drying—balance with ghee or dates
prakriti.pitta: highly pacifying (one of the best cooling and rejuvenative fruits for pitta)
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (astringent and light; excellent for metabolism, immunity, and clearing mucus)

name: Lotus Stem (Kamal Kakdi / Bhein)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Peel outer fibrous layer if needed. Slice into thin rounds or half-moons. Soak in water with a few drops of lemon juice to prevent browning. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, and a pinch of asafoetida for 8–10 minutes until tender-crisp. Can also be stir-fried with potatoes or added to curries.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and rough qualities); always cook with ghee and digestive spices like cumin or ginger
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (light, astringent, clears mucus and supports circulation)

name: Tinda (Apple Gourd / Indian Round Gourd)
instructions: Wash and peel the tinda. Cut in halves, remove seeds if mature, and slice or cube. Sauté in ghee with cumin seeds, turmeric, a pinch of asafoetida (hing), and a small amount of tomato or yogurt for 8–10 minutes until tender. Can also be cooked with potatoes or in a light curry. Avoid overcooking to retain shape and nutrients.

prakriti.vata: pacifying (moist and grounding when cooked with ghee)
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (cooling and soothing to the digestive tract)
prakriti.kapha: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (sweet quality); best balanced with ginger, black pepper, or cumin

name: Curry Leaves (Kadi Patta)
instructions: Wash and pat dry. Use whole in tempering (tadka)—heat ghee or oil, add mustard seeds, then curry leaves; let them sizzle 15–20 seconds until aromatic. Can also be finely chopped and added to chutneys, dals, or rice dishes. Best used fresh; dried leaves have reduced potency.

prakriti.vata: aggravating in excess (drying), but balancing in small amounts due to carminative properties; always use with ghee or oil
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (bitter + heating); use moderately, especially in summer or acidity
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (stimulates digestion, clears mucus, reduces ama and fat)

name: Arrowroot (Koypchini / Ararot)
instructions: Use as a gluten-free thickener: mix with cold water to make a slurry, then stir into soups, sauces, or kheer at the end of cooking. For digestive support, mix 1 tsp in warm water or herbal tea. Can also be used in pancakes or porridge. Avoid high-heat prolonged cooking to preserve properties.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating (dry quality); best combined with ghee, milk, or mashed banana if used as food
prakriti.pitta: highly pacifying (cooling, soothing to mucous membranes, excellent during acidity or diarrhea)
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, absorbent, helps dry excess moisture and mucus)

name: Olive (Jaitun)
instructions: Rinse briefly to reduce saltiness if using brined olives. Eat as is, or chop and add to salads, grain bowls, or cooked vegetable dishes. Best consumed in moderation. Avoid combining with milk or sweet fruits.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in small amounts (oily and grounding), but excess may increase ama; best with digestive spices
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (sour + heating; high in oleic acid—can increase acidity); avoid during pitta imbalance or summer
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, oily, increases mucus); consume very sparingly or avoid in kapha-dominant conditions

name: Lettuce (Salad Patta)
instructions: Wash thoroughly and pat dry. Best consumed raw in salads with lemon, olive oil, or a light dressing. For vata or kapha imbalance, lightly sauté in ghee with cumin and black pepper for 2–3 minutes. Avoid combining with dairy or heavy legumes.

prakriti.vata: aggravating (dry and rough qualities increase vata); avoid raw if vata is high—cook lightly with ghee
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (cooling and hydrating, especially romaine or butter lettuce)
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, low-calorie, clears mild stagnation)

name: Chilly (Green Chili / Hari Mirch)
instructions: Wash and use whole, sliced, or finely chopped. Add to tempering, curries, chutneys, or dals for heat and flavor. For reduced pungency, remove seeds. Use sparingly—especially in pitta imbalance or sensitive digestion.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in small amounts (stimulates digestion and circulation), but aggravating in excess (causes dryness and anxiety)
prakriti.pitta: strongly aggravating (intense heating and sharp qualities—increases acidity, inflammation, and irritability)
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for clearing mucus, boosting agni, and reducing stagnation)

name: Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia / Guduchi)
ingredients: 1 small piece fresh giloy stem (approx. 2-inch), chopped, or 1/4 tsp dried powder
instructions: Wash fresh stem thoroughly. Crush or chop finely. Boil in 1 cup water for 10–15 minutes to make a decoction (kadha). Strain and drink warm, preferably in the morning on empty stomach. Alternatively, mix dried powder in warm water or honey. Avoid long-term continuous use without guidance.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in moderate doses (supports immunity and nervous system), but may dry tissues in excess—balance with ghee or milk if needed
prakriti.pitta: pacifying (despite heating virya, its bitter rasa and sweet vipaka cool pitta long-term; excellent for fever and inflammation)
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (clears ama, boosts agni, and reduces toxins and congestion)

name: Jeera (Cumin Seeds)
instructions: Use whole for tempering—heat in ghee or oil until fragrant (15–20 seconds)—or dry roast and grind for powders. Can be steeped in hot water for 5–10 minutes to make a digestive tea. Best used fresh; store in airtight container away from light.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (carminative, reduces gas, kindles agni without overheating)
prakriti.pitta: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (heating), but beneficial in small amounts for digestion; avoid during high pitta or acidity
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (dries mucus, stimulates metabolism, clears stagnation)

name: Ajwain (Carom Seeds)
instructions: Use whole in tempering (tadka)—heat in ghee or oil until fragrant (10–15 seconds). Can be chewed raw after meals for digestion, or steeped in hot water for 5–7 minutes to make a soothing tea. Also added to doughs (e.g., paratha) to reduce gas.

prakriti.vata: highly pacifying (excellent for bloating, gas, and abdominal discomfort; kindles agni strongly)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (intense heating potency); use sparingly during summer, acidity, or inflammation
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (clears mucus, respiratory congestion, and sluggish digestion)

name: Clove (Laung)
instructions: Use whole in rice, curries, or teas—remove before eating. For toothache, chew gently or hold near affected area. To make clove tea, simmer 2–3 cloves in 1 cup water for 5–7 minutes. Do not use in excess—strong and heating.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in small doses (warming, analgesic, improves circulation), but aggravating in excess (drying)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (strong heating and sharp qualities—increases heat, acidity, and inflammation); avoid during pitta imbalance or summer
prakriti.kapha: highly pacifying (excellent for lung congestion, mucus, and low agni; antimicrobial and stimulating)

name: Mango (Aam)
ingredients: 1 small ripe mango, peeled and cubed (approx. 150g)
instructions: Eat fresh when fully ripe—ideally between meals, not with other foods. Avoid combining with milk, yogurt, or heavy meals. Raw (unripe) mango can be used in small amounts in cooked dishes like dal or kachumber with roasted cumin and black salt.

prakriti.vata: pacifying when ripe (grounding, nourishing); unripe is aggravating (drying, sour)
prakriti.pitta: ripe: neutral to slightly aggravating in excess (sweet + sour may increase acidity); unripe: strongly aggravating—avoid in summer or pitta imbalance
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, sweet, increases mucus); consume only occasionally in small amounts, preferably with ginger, black pepper, or cardamom

name: Kiwi (Kiwi Fruit)
instructions: Wash thoroughly. Peel skin (or eat with skin if organic and well-scrubbed). Eat fresh between meals—ideally in the morning. Avoid combining with dairy, melons, or heavy foods. Best consumed alone for optimal digestion.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating in excess (sour can stimulate vata; rough skin may increase dryness); best eaten ripe and peeled
prakriti.pitta: aggravating in excess (high acidity and sour taste increase pitta); limit during summer or acidity
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, sour, and metabolic—helps clear mucus and boost digestion)

name: Dragon Fruit (Pitaya)
ingredients: 1 medium dragon fruit, peeled and cubed (approx. 150g)
instructions: Cut in half and scoop out the flesh with a spoon, or peel and slice. Eat fresh between meals—preferably in the morning. Avoid combining with dairy, bananas, or heavy foods. Best consumed alone for light digestion.

prakriti.vata: slightly aggravating in excess (dry and light qualities may increase vata); balance with a few drops of ghee or eat with soaked almonds if vata is high
prakriti.pitta: highly pacifying (cooling, hydrating, and soothing—excellent during summer or inflammation)
prakriti.kapha: pacifying (light, low in sugar relative to other fruits—supports detox and metabolism)

name: Jackfruit (Kathal)
ingredients: 1 cup ripe jackfruit bulbs or young unripe pieces (approx. 150g)
instructions: For **ripe jackfruit**: Peel, deseed, and eat fresh—sweet and aromatic. For **unripe (young) jackfruit**: Peel outer skin, remove core, cut into cubes, and cook in curries or stir-fries like a vegetable (sauté in ghee with turmeric, cumin, and hing for 10–15 minutes). Never combine ripe jackfruit with dairy.
allergy_info: latex (cross-reactivity with banana, avocado, or birch pollen)

prakriti.vata: pacifying when ripe (nourishing and grounding); unripe is aggravating (hard to digest, dry)
prakriti.pitta: aggravating (heating potency—especially ripe); avoid during summer, acidity, or inflammation
prakriti.kapha: aggravating (heavy, sweet, increases mucus); consume only occasionally in small amounts, preferably unripe and well-spiced

name: Aloe Vera (Ghritkumari)
instructions: Wash a thick aloe leaf thoroughly. Peel and carefully scrape out the clear inner gel—avoid the yellow latex layer (aloin), which is bitter and laxative. Consume fresh gel on an empty stomach, mixed with a few drops of honey or lemon. For skin use, apply pure gel topically. Do not consume daily for more than 7–10 days without guidance.

prakriti.vata: pacifying in small doses (moisturizing for dryness), but excess may cause coldness or sluggishness—use with warming spices like ginger if needed
prakriti.pitta: highly pacifying (one of the best cooling herbs—excellent for digestion, skin, liver, and inflammation)
prakriti.kapha: aggravating in excess (heavy, slimy, increases mucus); use only short-term and in small amounts during kapha imbalance
